skill.name: controlnet-pose
skill.description: Route pose-conditioned image and video generation requests to RunComfy models through the runcomfy CLI. The skill helps choose between Kling motion transfer, Wan animation, and Z-Image ControlNet LoRA based on the user request.
skill.summary: Generate pose-controlled images and videos with RunComfy model routes.
skill.author: doany-ai

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported external command, network, filesystem, weak crypto, and reconnaissance patterns. AI review confirms legitimate external CLI, network, and token-storage risks for a RunComfy cloud generation skill, while weak crypto and reconnaissance alerts appear to be false positives from model names, line text, and exit-code documentation. No prompt injection attempt or malicious intent was found in SKILL.md.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Execution Through RunComfy
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The skill instructs agents to use the runcomfy CLI for generation jobs. This is expected for the skill, but it executes installed local CLI code and can create output files, so users must trust the installed package and command arguments.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented workflow directly invokes the runcomfy CLI and the skill limits Bash scope to runcomfy. The behavior is intentional and bounded, but external command execution remains a real operational risk.

medium_findings.title: Outbound Cloud Processing of Media References
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The skill sends prompts and user-provided image, video, or control URLs to RunComfy model endpoints. This is core functionality, but it can expose media locations and prompt content to a third-party cloud service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly describes RunComfy Model API calls and an allowlist of RunComfy endpoints. The risk is privacy and data handling, not evidence of malicious exfiltration.

low_findings.title: API Token Stored in User Config Directory
low_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The skill documents that runcomfy login stores an API token under the user home configuration directory with mode 0600. This is normal CLI behavior, but users should understand that a local credential file is created.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token storage path and permission mode are explicitly documented. The stated 0600 mode lowers severity, but local credential storage is still a valid filesystem risk.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Crypto and Reconnaissance Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The weak cryptography alerts align with model names, descriptive numbers, and exit-code documentation rather than cryptographic code. The reconnaissance alerts occur in model selection guidance and do not instruct system probing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review of the surrounding text shows no hashing, encryption, host discovery, identity checks, or system enumeration. The matches are semantic noise from documentation text.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Mostly Documentation and Service Links
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for most URL detections. The hardcoded URLs point to RunComfy documentation, catalog pages, and example CDN placeholders. They do not show hidden endpoints or unrelated data collection destinations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The links are visible marketplace, documentation, model, tutorial, and related-skill URLs. The only meaningful network concern is the expected RunComfy cloud processing described separately.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Access Limited to runcomfy Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill scope permits Bash execution for runcomfy commands. This bounded external command pattern is necessary for the skill but should be treated as a review point during publication.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file consistently frames command execution around the runcomfy CLI. No evidence was found for arbitrary shell execution outside that tool boundary.

dangerous_patterns.title: Third-Party Asset URLs Used as Model Inputs
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill accepts user-provided image, video, and control URLs as inputs to remote generation. This can be safe when user-approved, but it creates privacy and indirect prompt-injection considerations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples and security section explicitly describe URL-based media inputs and call those URLs untrusted. The skill also gives reasonable mitigations by requiring user-provided URLs.

content.value_statement: Pose and motion control requires choosing the correct model route and input format. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to use RunComfy routes for pose-conditioned image and video generation.
actual_capabilities: Routes video pose-transfer requests to Kling Motion Control Pro or Standard.
Routes image pose-conditioning requests to Z-Image Turbo ControlNet LoRA.
Explains when to use Wan 2-2 Animate for stylized character animation.
Shows the required input fields for reference videos, character images, and control images.
Points users to RunComfy ComfyUI workflows for multi-condition ControlNet stacks.
Documents RunComfy CLI exit codes and security boundaries.
limitations: Requires the runcomfy CLI and a valid RunComfy login or token.
Requires hosted URLs for reference videos, character images, or control images.
Does not generate OpenPose or DWPose control images by itself.
Multi-condition ComfyUI workflows are described but not executed through the CLI route.

use_cases.title: Transfer Reference Motion to a Character
use_cases.description: Use a performance video and a target character image to create a new motion-controlled video.

use_cases.title: Create Pose-Locked Character Art
use_cases.description: Use an OpenPose, DWPose, depth, or canny control image to keep a generated character in a specific stance.

use_cases.title: Select a RunComfy Control Route
use_cases.description: Compare Kling, Wan, Z-Image, and ComfyUI workflow options before running a pose-conditioned generation task.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Pose-Controlled Image
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this pose reference to generate a character image. Help me choose the right RunComfy route and list the inputs I need.

prompt_templates.title: Transfer Motion to a New Character
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a reference performance video and a target character image. Use the best RunComfy motion-control route and prepare the generation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Pose and Motion Models
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare the RunComfy options for this request: video motion transfer, stylized character animation, and still-image pose control.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Multi-Condition ControlNet Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: I need pose plus depth or canny control. Identify whether the CLI route is enough or whether I should open a RunComfy ComfyUI workflow.

output_examples.input: A user provides a dance reference video and a target character image.
output_examples.output: The skill selects Kling Motion Control Pro, explains the required hosted media URLs, and describes where the generated video will be downloaded.

output_examples.input: A user provides an OpenPose skeleton and asks for a samurai image in the same stance.
output_examples.output: The skill selects Z-Image Turbo ControlNet LoRA and asks for a hosted control image URL and final visual prompt.

output_examples.input: A user asks for pose, depth, and canny conditioning together.
output_examples.output: The skill explains that a RunComfy ComfyUI workflow is the better route because the documented CLI endpoints cover single-condition cases.

best_practices: Use only media URLs that the user explicitly provided or approved.
Choose the route by output type first: video motion transfer or still-image pose control.
Keep reference media clean, well-framed, and limited to the subject needed for the generation.
anti_patterns: Do not pass private or unapproved media URLs to a cloud generation endpoint.
Do not use the still-image ControlNet route for video motion transfer.
Do not expect the CLI route to run complex multi-condition ComfyUI workflows.

faq.question: What does this skill generate?
faq.answer: It helps generate pose-conditioned images and motion-controlled videos through RunComfy model routes.

faq.question: Does it require a RunComfy account?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow expects the runcomfy CLI to be installed and authenticated with a login or token.

faq.question: Can it create OpenPose or DWPose maps?
faq.answer: No. The skill expects control images to be prepared separately and provided as hosted URLs.

faq.question: Which route should I use for video pose transfer?
faq.answer: Use Kling Motion Control Pro for final video pose transfer, or Standard for cheaper drafts.

faq.question: Which route should I use for still images?
faq.answer: Use Z-Image Turbo ControlNet LoRA for pose, depth, or canny-conditioned image generation.

faq.question: What security precautions matter most?
faq.answer: Use trusted CLI installation methods, avoid unapproved media URLs, and protect the RunComfy token stored by the CLI.
